title: Creating Your First Unreal Project
learningTarget: I can create and save a new Unreal Engine project using the correct class setup, project name, and starter content settings.

bellRinger.prompt: What kinds of digital worlds have you seen in games, movies, architecture, or VR?

vocabulary.definition: A real-time 3D creation tool used for games, film, visualization, and interactive media.
The launcher used to install and open Unreal Engine.
The startup window where users open recent projects or create new ones.
A project template that starts with minimal prebuilt content.
Sample assets that can be added to a new project for practice.
Unreal visual scripting used instead of writing C++ for many beginner interactions.

slides.title: Lesson 01 - Creating Your First Unreal Project

assignment.title: First Unreal Project Setup
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of project browser or open Unreal project
Screenshot or written confirmation showing correct project name

exitTicket: What setting should be enabled if we want starter assets available in the project?

title: Unreal Interface Tour
learningTarget: I can identify the main Unreal interface panels and explain how each one supports building and organizing a 3D world.

bellRinger.prompt: Which software interface have you used that felt complicated at first, and what helped you learn it?

vocabulary.definition: The main window that shows the 3D world and lets users select or manipulate objects.
The row of tools used to add objects, switch modes, play, and access common actions.
A list of objects in the current level, useful for selecting, organizing, hiding, and grouping.
The panel that shows editable properties for the selected object.
The asset browser that can open from the bottom of the editor, often with Ctrl+Space.
The docked version of the Content Drawer that shows project folders and assets.

slides.title: Lesson 02 - Unreal Interface Tour

assignment.title: Unreal Interface Map
evidenceRequired: Annotated screenshot or form response identifying viewport, toolbar, outliner, details panel, and content drawer

exitTicket: Which panel shows the selected object's editable settings?

title: Viewport Navigation
learningTarget: I can navigate the Unreal viewport confidently using mouse controls, keyboard movement, focus, view modes, show flags, and game view.

bellRinger.prompt: Why might navigation controls matter before building anything in a 3D scene?

vocabulary.definition: The controls used to move the editor camera around the 3D world.
A first-person style movement method used while holding the right mouse button.
The setting that controls how fast the viewport camera moves.
The F key action that snaps the viewport to the selected object.
A viewport display mode such as Lit, Unlit, or Wireframe.
A viewport option toggled with G that hides editor-only widgets and shows what the player would see.

slides.title: Lesson 03 - Viewport Navigation

assignment.title: Viewport Navigation Scavenger Hunt
evidenceRequired: Screenshots from at least three different views or camera positions
One screenshot showing game view or a different view mode

exitTicket: What does pressing G toggle in the viewport?

title: Moving, Rotating, and Scaling Objects
learningTarget: I can build a simple starter scene by moving, rotating, scaling, duplicating, and placing objects from the toolbar and Content Drawer.

bellRinger.prompt: What is the difference between moving an object and changing the camera view?

vocabulary.definition: Move an object along one or more axes.
Turn an object around an axis.
Change the size or proportions of an object.
The colored handles used to move, rotate, or scale selected objects.
A setting that moves, rotates, or scales objects in set increments.
Moving or rotating based on the selected object direction instead of the world direction.

slides.title: Lesson 04 - Moving, Rotating, and Scaling Objects

assignment.title: Starter Scene Transform Challenge
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of completed simple scene
Short reflection naming at least three tools or shortcuts used

exitTicket: What are the W, E, and R shortcuts used for in Unreal?

title: Unreal Reboot and Production Expectations
learningTarget: I can reconnect with Unreal basics and explain the expectations for a production-style environment project.

bellRinger.prompt: What makes an environment feel like a finished world instead of a practice scene?

vocabulary.definition: An organized process for planning, building, reviewing, and finishing a creative project.

slides.title: Q3 Lesson 01 - Unreal Reboot and Production Expectations

assignment.title: Q3 Production Project Setup
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of the Q3 project setup
Written production goal for the castle environment

exitTicket: What is one production habit you need to maintain this quarter?

title: Environment Planning
learningTarget: I can plan a fantasy/castle environment using references, constraints, and a clear visual goal.

bellRinger.prompt: What details help an audience understand where a scene takes place?

vocabulary.definition: A collection of images and notes used to guide the look and direction of a project.

slides.title: Q3 Lesson 02 - Environment Planning

assignment.title: Castle Environment Plan
evidenceRequired: Reference board or planning document
Simple layout sketch or written scene plan

exitTicket: What is your main focal point for the castle environment?

title: Third-Person Project Setup
learningTarget: I can set up a third-person Unreal project for environment walkthrough testing.

bellRinger.prompt: Why is it useful to test an environment from a player's point of view?

vocabulary.definition: An Unreal project template that includes a controllable character and camera setup.

slides.title: Q3 Lesson 03 - Third-Person Project Setup

assignment.title: Third-Person Walkthrough Setup
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of the playable character in the project
Short note explaining why player scale matters

exitTicket: How does player scale affect environment design?

title: Landscape Blockout
learningTarget: I can block out terrain forms that support a castle environment layout.

bellRinger.prompt: What terrain features could make a castle feel protected, isolated, or dramatic?

vocabulary.definition: An early rough version of a scene used to test layout, scale, and composition.
A terrain system used to create large ground surfaces in Unreal.

slides.title: Q3 Lesson 04 - Landscape Blockout

assignment.title: Castle Landscape Blockout
evidenceRequired: Top-down screenshot of the landscape blockout
Player-view screenshot showing the route or focal point

exitTicket: What part of your blockout guides the viewer toward the castle?

title: Quiz 1 and First Scene Cleanup

video.note: Review range only; no new required tutorial segment.

learningTarget: I can show what I remember from Lessons 01-04 and improve my first Unreal scene using feedback, organization, and stronger evidence screenshots.

bellRinger.prompt: Which Unreal tool or shortcut from Lessons 01-04 helped you the most so far, and why?

vocabulary.definition: A focused check of earlier skills and vocabulary so you know what is solid and what still needs practice.
Information from a teacher, peer, rubric, or self-check that helps you decide what to improve.
A purposeful change that makes the work clearer, stronger, or more complete.
A screenshot, file, or written explanation that proves a skill was used.
A polished image of work that could be used later to show growth or achievement.

slides.title: Lesson 05 - Quiz 1 and First Scene Cleanup

assignment.title: First Scene Cleanup and Evidence Upgrade
evidenceRequired: Before screenshot, if available.
After screenshot.
Short reflection naming at least three tools or shortcuts.
Optional quiz completion screenshot or teacher checkoff.

exitTicket: What is one improvement you made to your first Unreal scene today?

title: Exposure and Post-Process

video.note: Covers auto exposure, a hut/dark-space test, manual exposure, post-process volume settings, bloom, vignette, saturation, and Infinite Extent Unbound.

learningTarget: I can use a post-process volume to control exposure and camera-based visual effects in an Unreal scene.

bellRinger.prompt: Why does a camera or your eyes sometimes need time to adjust when moving from a bright place to a dark place?

vocabulary.definition: How bright or dark the rendered scene appears to the camera.
A camera behavior that automatically adjusts brightness, similar to eyes adapting between dark and bright areas.
An Unreal volume that applies camera and image effects after the scene is rendered.
A glow effect around bright areas of the image.
A darkening or shading effect around the edges of the image.
The strength or intensity of colors in the image.
A Post Process Volume setting that makes the effect apply to the entire level instead of only inside the volume.
The exposure measurement mode used to decide how scene brightness should be handled.

slides.title: Lesson 06 - Exposure and Post-Process

assignment.title: Post-Process Exposure Test
evidenceRequired: Screenshot showing auto exposure/problem situation.
Screenshot showing manual exposure/post-process result.
Short reflection explaining what changed.

exitTicket: What setting allows a post-process volume to affect the whole level?

title: Materials Basics

video.note: Covers material example setup, starter materials, creating a material, the material graph, Base Color, Roughness, Metallic, PBR, Apply/Save, and simple texture-driven variation.

learningTarget: I can create a basic Unreal material and control how it looks using base color, roughness, metallic, and simple texture-driven variation.

bellRinger.prompt: How can you tell whether a surface is metal, plastic, stone, wood, or paint just by looking at it?

vocabulary.definition: An Unreal asset that defines how a surface looks when rendered.
The node-based workspace used to build and edit a material.
A block in the graph that stores or processes information before sending it to a material input.
The main visible color of a material before lighting effects are applied.
A value that controls how sharp or blurry reflections appear on a surface.
A value that controls whether a surface behaves like metal or a non-metal.
Physically based rendering, a workflow that uses realistic material values such as metallic and roughness.
A single number value, often used for settings such as Roughness or Metallic.
A multi-number value often used to represent RGB color.
The material editor action that updates the material so changes appear in the level.

slides.title: Lesson 07 - Materials Basics

assignment.title: First Custom Material
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of the material graph.
Screenshot of the material applied to an object.
Short reflection explaining base color, roughness, and metallic.

exitTicket: What happens if you change a material but forget to press Apply?

title: Texture Maps and Normal Maps

video.note: Covers importing texture maps, Base Color maps, Roughness maps, Normal maps, sRGB settings, mask/non-color texture settings, normal map compression, material connections, Texture Coordinate, Multiply, tiling/scale, and optional color tint. Ends before material instances.

learningTarget: I can import texture maps and connect base color, roughness, and normal maps correctly to build a more believable material.

bellRinger.prompt: What is the difference between color detail and surface depth detail?

vocabulary.definition: An image file used by a material to control part of the surface appearance.
A color texture that provides the main visible color detail of a material.
A grayscale texture that controls which parts of a surface look shiny or rough.
A texture that fakes small bumps and surface direction changes so lighting reacts with more detail.
A color interpretation setting that should be on for color textures and off for non-color data maps.
A non-color texture that stores control information, often in black, white, or grayscale values.
An import setting that tells Unreal how to treat and optimize a texture file.
A node that controls how texture UVs are read by a material.
The 2D coordinate layout used to place a texture on a 3D surface.
How often a texture repeats across a surface.

slides.title: Lesson 08 - Texture Maps and Normal Maps

assignment.title: Texture Map Material Build
evidenceRequired: Screenshot of imported texture assets.
Screenshot of material graph connections.
Screenshot of material applied in the level.
Short reflection explaining what the normal map adds.

exitTicket: Why should sRGB usually be turned off for roughness maps and normal maps?
